course_identity.notes: Matched CSC 212 (Data Structures) Spring 2025 offering; instructor Marco Alvarez. Syllabus uploaded PDF used as ground truth.

grading_categories.name: Homework
grading_categories.notes: Individual unless stated; autograded via Gradescope; new assignment roughly every 6–9 days.

grading_categories.name: Midterm 1
grading_categories.notes: Open-book (printed/handwritten notes only); no electronic devices. Covers linked lists through stacks/queues.

grading_categories.name: Midterm 2
grading_categories.notes: Open-book; covers trees, heaps, and hash tables.

grading_categories.name: Final Exam
grading_categories.notes: Cumulative. Open-book. Covers all material with emphasis on graphs and algorithm analysis.

grading_categories.name: Participation / Labs
grading_categories.notes: Weekly lab attendance required; up to 5–10% extra credit tied to participation (not included in base 100%).

course_profile.late_policy: No late submissions accepted for assignments.
course_profile.attendance_policy: Lab sessions are structured (lecture segments, live coding, exercises); attendance expected per syllabus.
course_profile.drop_rules: Follow URI academic calendar and department policies for drops/withdrawals.
required_tools: C++ development environment (course-specific toolchain per instructor)
course_profile.course_summary: CSC 212 covers fundamental data structures and algorithms—lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, and hash tables—with implementation emphasis in C++ and analysis using asymptotic notation.
course_profile.workflow_notes: Lectures TR 9:30–10:45 AM; multiple Wednesday lab sections. Prerequisites: CSC 211 (C- or better) and MTH 180 or Computer Engineering major status. Office hours: Tyler Hall CS lounge (schedule TBD). Academic integrity policy prohibits uncredited AI use; AI is allowed if cited and you understand all submitted work.

resources.title: URI CS Department — official course hub
resources.reason: Official CS department page for policies, labs, and advising context.

resources.title: VisuAlgo — interactive data structure visualizations
resources.reason: Animated step-through of BSTs, heaps, graphs, and sorting algorithms — directly aligned with CSC 212 topics.

resources.title: GeeksforGeeks — Data Structures & Algorithms
resources.reason: Quick reference C++ implementations with complexity analysis — good supplement for homework.

resources.title: OpenDSA — Virginia Tech interactive textbook
resources.reason: Undergraduate DS textbook with embedded exercises matching CSC 212 curriculum.

resources.title: cppreference.com — C++ STL containers
resources.reason: Authoritative reference for std::vector, std::list, std::map, std::unordered_map — all used in CSC 212.

resources.title: CS50 Data Structures lecture (Harvard OpenCourseWare)
resources.reason: Free recorded lectures with strong visual explanations of pointers and linked lists.

resources.title: MIT 6.006 Introduction to Algorithms (OpenCourseWare)
resources.reason: Lecture notes and problem sets on BSTs, hashing, graphs — deeper theory supplement for exam prep.

resources.title: r/learnprogramming — C++ data structures
resources.reason: Active community for debugging help and conceptual questions.

resources.title: GitHub — marco-a-alvarez / csc212
resources.reason: Official course GitHub org with starter code and lab skeletons.

resources.title: YouTube — Back To Back SWE (trees & graphs)
resources.reason: High-quality whiteboard videos on trees, graphs, BFS/DFS — exam prep friendly.

detected_tools.evidence: Listed in syllabus as platform for discussion, polls, quizzes, and collaboration.
Syllabus specifies assignment submission and autograding through Gradescope.
Syllabus references virtual meetings and support sessions.
C++ development environment required; popular toolchain for CSC courses.
Memory-safe C++ is emphasized; Valgrind is standard tool in URI CS labs.
detected_tools.purpose: Memory leak detection for C++ assignments

student_signal.workload: Moderate to high — steady coding assignments on a 6–9 day cadence plus three exams.
student_signal.difficulty: Typical second-year CS data structures pace; pointer-heavy and proof-style analysis on exams.
student_signal.grading_style: Strict no-late policy on homework; majority of grade from exams (65%).
common_pitfalls: Starting homework within 2 days of the deadline — autograded assignments punish edge cases
Not building printed cheat sheet early — open-book exams reward prepared students
Skipping lab sections — participation can add 5–10% extra credit
Ignoring memory leaks — C++ pointer bugs compound across assignments
Not reading output spec — Gradescope diffs are exact and whitespace matters
key_warnings: Exams prohibit electronic devices; open notes only if printed/handwritten
Academic integrity explicitly calls out uncredited AI use
positive_signals: Transparent grading scale published on syllabus
Multiple Wednesday lab section times to choose from
AI use permitted with citation and understanding requirement
Clear support pathways (AEC, DAI, religious observance process)
Starter code provided via GitHub org for most assignments
student_signal.summary: Structured, exam-weighted course with firm deadlines. Strong fit if you keep up weekly and budget time for open-book exam prep without relying on devices.

obligations.title: Midterm 1
obligations.conflict_note: Open-book (printed/handwritten only). Covers arrays through stacks/queues.

obligations.title: Midterm 2
obligations.conflict_note: Open-book. Covers trees, heaps, and hash tables.

obligations.title: Final Exam
obligations.conflict_note: Cumulative, open-book. Confirm exact slot with URI final exam schedule.
